attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old man remains in ICU seven days after a subarachnoid haemorrhage from a berry aneurysm rupture. He is registered as an organ donor, and this is confirmed with his next of kin. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

He was given a bolus of midazolam 24 hours ago for twitching movements. He has no signs of improvement during his admission, and has remained unconscious (GCS 3) and ventilated with no respiratory effort, including during periods where sedation is weaned. On examination, he appears unwell with pulse rate 85/min, blood pressure 105/55 mmHg (MAP 70 mmHg on low dose noradrenaline), respiratory rate ventilated, SpO2 98% on mechanical ventilation, and temperature 35.8°C. On neurological examination, there are positive biceps and brachioradialis reflexes, and the vestibulo-ocular reflex is present.

His loved ones indicate he would intend to be an organ donor, if brain death was formally determined.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following examination findings precludes the diagnosis of brain death in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
Neurological determination of death (previously termed 'brain death') is a rigorous process designed to determine whether there is permanent loss of all brain functions.

The ANZICS guidelines provide recommendations to make this diagnosis. Neurological determination of death cannot be conducted without clinical or neuroimaging evidence of sufficient intracranial pathology. In cases of hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy (e.g. following cardiac arrest) clinical history may be sufficient and neuroimaging is not necessarily required.

The patient in our stem has an intra-cranial cause for an unresponsive coma, and neuroimaging would undoubtedly have been performed during their admission. This imaging must demonstrate pathology affecting the whole brain (not just the brain stem, or cerebral hemispheres) to invoke the potential of neurological determination of death.

To perform clinical testing for neurological determination of death, certain pre-conditions must be met. These preconditions include: normothermia (≥35°C), normotension (SBP≥90 mmHg, MAP ≥60 mmHg), exclusion of effects of sedative medications, absence of severe electrolyte metabolic or endocrine disturbances, with further pre-conditions outlined in the ANZICS guidelines.

A minimum 4-hour observation period should also occur prior to clinical testing. During this period, the patient must have a GCS of 3, non-reactive pupils, absent cough/tracheal reflex and apparent apnoea on the ventilator, i.e. there must be all three of: absence of responsiveness, absence of brainstem reflexes and absence of breathing.

The vestibulo-ocular reflex is a brainstem reflex. If this reflex is present, it would exclude neurological determination of death on clinical examination.

Positive deep tendon reflexes, such as the biceps and brachioradialis reflexes, are spinal reflexes and may be present in up to 50% of patients with permanent loss of brain function. As such, the presence of these reflexes would not exclude the diagnosis of brain death.

Blood pressure requiring vasopressor support does not preclude the diagnosis of brain death, provided the minimum blood pressure thresholds are maintained.

Normothermia (≥35°C) is a pre-condition for the clinical diagnosis of brain death, and thus hypothermia (<35°C) prevents the diagnosis of brain death, however, 35.8°C would be considered normothermic and therefore would not preclude the diagnosis. If the patient has been hypothermic for >6 hours, the clinical examination to determine brain death should be delayed for at least 24 hours after rewarming.

Recent midazolam administration could preclude the diagnosis of brain death as sedative medications must be excluded before clinical testing can be performed. However, given that the midazolam was administered 24 hours ago as a single bolus for twitching, and considering midazolam's relatively short half-life, this would likely not preclude testing at this time point, though institutional protocols may vary regarding the required washout period.
learningPoints: Neurological determination of death (brain death) is a permanent loss of all brain functions.
The presence of cranial nerve reflexes would preclude a diagnosis of brain death.
Deep tendon reflexes are spinal reflexes and may be present in patients with brain death.
references: The Australian and New Zealand Intensive Care Society. Statement on Death and Organ Donation. Edition 4.1. 2021 [cited 2024 March 23]. Available from: https://www.donatelife.gov.au/sites/default/files/2022-01/anzics-statement-on-death-and-organ-donation-4.1.pdf
